Hazmat Handling.clue: This is where Safety Data Sheets (SDS) should be kept for shop chemicals.
choices: Locked in the supervisor's office
In a location accessible to all shop personnel during all work shifts work shifts
Filed with the manufacturer only
Discarded after opening the chemical
Hazmat Handling.explanation: SDS must be accessible to all personnel at all times, per OSHA. Locked or filed-offsite storage defeats the purpose. Discarding them violates the regulation.

Hazmat Handling.clue: When handling solvents and chemicals, this is the most important first step for a new technician.
choices: Smell the chemical to identify it
Read the Safety Data Sheet (SDS) to understand hazards
Apply the chemical quickly to see how it works
Mix chemicals to see which is stronger
Hazmat Handling.explanation: Reading the SDS is the required first step. Smelling is dangerous (some chemicals are toxic at low exposure). Applying without precaution invites injury. Mixing chemicals can produce violent reactions.

Hazmat Handling.clue: This is the most correct response when a technician feels dizzy from a strong solvent smell while cleaning boards.
choices: Continue working and ignore the symptoms
Leave the area immediately for fresh air
Open the chemical container wider for better access
Sit down next to the chemical and rest
Hazmat Handling.explanation: Leave for fresh air immediately. Continuing work or resting near the source both worsen exposure. Opening the chemical wider increases exposure.

Hazmat Handling.clue: In a GA repair environment, proper disposal of hazardous materials typically involves this.
choices: Pouring them down the shop drain
Placing them in the regular trash
Burning them in a fire barrel
Collecting them in designated labeled containers by category and delivering them to a certified hazardous-waste recycler or disposal service
Hazmat Handling.explanation: Proper hazmat disposal uses designated containers and certified disposal services. Drain, trash, and burning are all environmentally illegal and hazardous to personnel.

PPE & Protection.clue: When working on aircraft systems that emit RF signals, this is one of the biggest safety concerns.
choices: The aircraft might run out of fuel
Personnel exposure to potentially harmful RF energy
The aircraft paint might fade
The RF signals disturbing nearby Wi-Fi networks
PPE & Protection.explanation: Personnel RF exposure is a real health and safety concern — eyes and reproductive organs are particularly vulnerable. Fuel, paint, and Wi-Fi concerns are not primary shop-safety issues with RF systems.

PPE & Protection.clue: This describes a safe practice around moving rotorcraft.
choices: Walk under the rotor disc regardless of rotor speed
Approach from directly behind
Run quickly through the rotor disc area

Maintain eye contact with the pilot, stay in designated safe zones clear of the rotor disc, approach only when signaled, stay low when entering the rotor area, and avoid the tail rotor region entirely
PPE & Protection.explanation: Eye contact, safe zones, correct approach direction, staying low, and avoiding the tail rotor are all established safe-approach practices. Walking under, approaching from behind, or running through the disc area are all dangerous.

Fire & Emergency.clue: Class C fires are commonly associated with this.
choices: Energized electrical equipment such as motors, wiring, and avionics
Ordinary combustibles like wood and paper
Flammable liquids like gasoline and solvents
Cooking oils and greases
Fire & Emergency.explanation: Class A is ordinary combustibles; Class B is flammable liquids; Class C is energized electrical; Class K is cooking oils.

Fire & Emergency.clue: When checking a fire extinguisher's readiness in a GA avionics shop, a technician should verify these items.
choices: Only the color of the extinguisher body
Pressure gauge in the green zone, intact safety pin with tamper seal, no visible damage, current inspection tag, and correct mounting location
Only the tag date
Only that the extinguisher exists in the shop
Fire & Emergency.explanation: A proper readiness check covers multiple items. Surface-only or presence-only checks miss common failure modes — low pressure, missing pin, physical damage, expired inspection.

Fire & Emergency.clue: When a small screw is dropped behind a panel during avionics installation, this is what should be done immediately.
choices: Continue the installation and retrieve the screw later if convenient
Replace the screw with a new one without retrieving the old one
Stop work, locate and retrieve the screw before continuing
Assume the screw will remain harmless and ignore it
Fire & Emergency.explanation: Immediate retrieval is required. Delaying or ignoring creates a FOD hazard that can cause in-flight shorts, control jams, or equipment damage. Simply replacing the screw does not remove the dropped one.

Fire & Emergency.clue: This is the immediate, correct first aid response when encountering an electrical shock victim.
choices: Immediately grab the victim to pull them away
Pour water on the victim to cool them
FIRST ensure the power is off (never touch the victim while in contact with a live source)
Ignore the situation and continue working
Fire & Emergency.explanation: Cut power FIRST. Touching a still-energized victim can kill the rescuer. Then call 911 and provide CPR/AED as needed. Water could conduct electricity if the source is still live; ignoring is never acceptable.

Fire & Emergency.clue: You detect a burning insulation odor in an avionics rack. This is your first action.
choices: Continue working and see if the odor goes away
De-energize the affected circuit(s) — pull the appropriate circuit breakers or turn off the master switch — before investigating the source
Spray water on the avionics rack
Leave the aircraft and hope the problem resolves
Fire & Emergency.explanation: De-energize first. Continuing, ignoring, or spraying water on live electronics are all unsafe. Once de-energized, the source can be investigated safely.

Shop Procedures.clue: This best describes a 'near-miss' in the context of shop safety.
choices: An unplanned event that did not result in injury
Any minor injury that required first aid
A customer complaint about service
A tool that was temporarily misplaced
Shop Procedures.explanation: A near-miss is a no-consequence warning event that had the potential for bad consequences. It is distinct from actual minor injuries and unrelated to customer service or tool misplacement.

ESD Prevention.clue: This practice best prevents ESD damage to sensitive avionics.
choices: Wearing wool clothing for static comfort
Walking on carpet before touching components
Handling components with bare hands only
Using a grounded wrist strap, a grounded ESD mat, and keeping components in antistatic packaging until installation
ESD Prevention.explanation: Grounded wrist strap, grounded ESD mat, and antistatic packaging together protect against ESD. Wool and carpet generate static. Bare hands alone without grounding offer no protection.

ESD Prevention.clue: This is the most effective way to prevent FOD in an avionics shop.
choices: A layered approach: tool accountability, workspace discipline, hardware tracking, and shop-wide FOD awareness with regular walkdowns
A once-a-year deep clean
Relying on individual technicians' diligence alone
Installing more trash cans
ESD Prevention.explanation: FOD prevention is systemic, not individual. Layered defenses (accountability, discipline, tracking, awareness) catch FOD incidents before they reach the aircraft. Once-a-year cleaning is too infrequent. Individual diligence alone misses some incidents.

final.clue: Skydrol (phosphate ester hydraulic fluid), often encountered in large aircraft bays, requires immediate cleaning with soap and water upon skin contact because it is highly corrosive and causes what primary physical symptom?
choices: Instant freezing of tissue
Intense burning sensation similar to chemical burns
Painless numbness
Immediate blistering
final.explanation: Skydrol is notorious for causing intense, painful burning sensations upon contact with skin and, particularly, the eyes. Immediate flushing with water is the required first aid.

final.clue: Before approaching the radome of a powered-up transport category aircraft on the ramp, you must verify what system is pinned or disabled to prevent potentially lethal RF exposure?
choices: The Weather Radar
The TCAS transmitters
The VHF Comm antennas
The GPS receiver
final.explanation: Weather radar systems emit high-power non-ionizing radiation that can cause severe internal burns. They must be off or disabled via pin before personnel work near the nose of the aircraft.

final.clue: When working on highly sensitive avionics LRUs, an ESD wrist strap must be worn. What internal component is built into the wrist strap wire to protect the technician from electrocution if they touch a live power source?
choices: A 1 Amp fuse
A 1 Megohm resistor
A grounding busbar
A diode
final.explanation: A 1MΩ resistor is built into the strap. It allows static electricity to safely bleed to ground, but limits the current to a harmless level (milliamps) if the technician touches 115V AC.
